title: Bank Statement Analysis for Veterinary Clinics and Animal Hospitals
meta: Vet clinics combine cash-pay services, pet insurance, and financing partners. Learn how lenders analyze veterinary practice bank statements for equipment and expansion loans.
content:
Veterinary Practices: Healthcare Economics, Cash-Pay Reality

Veterinary medicine has the clinical profile of healthcare but the payment profile of cash-pay retail. Unlike human medicine, there is no dominant insurance reimbursement system — most revenue is collected at time of service, which produces clean, fundable bank statements.

How Vet Revenue Flows

  • Card settlements: Daily card batches at point of service; high average ticket for surgeries and diagnostics.
  • Pet-insurance reimbursements: Usually paid to the owner, not the clinic — so insurance rarely clutters the clinic's deposits.
  • Financing partner deposits: CareCredit and Scratchpay disburse to the clinic for financed procedures — recognize these as revenue, not anomalies.
  • Boarding, grooming, and pharmacy: Recurring ancillary revenue.

Equipment-Intensive and Recession-Resistant

Vet clinics invest heavily in diagnostic and surgical equipment (digital radiography, ultrasound, anesthesia, lab analyzers), so scheduled equipment-finance payments are normal and positive. Demand is also resilient — pet spending holds up through downturns, a strength lenders should weight.

What Lenders Focus On

  • Average monthly card settlements and growth trend
  • CareCredit/Scratchpay deposits recognized as legitimate revenue
  • Equipment-finance obligations vs. cash flow
  • Near-zero NSF for an established practice

Bottom Line

Veterinary clinics are excellent lending candidates: cash-pay, recession-resistant, and high-ticket. StatementScrub flags financing-partner deposits as revenue and produces clean income figures for equipment and expansion loans.
